learning_outcomes.description: Analyse the foundational principles, sources, and institutional structures of diverse legal traditions across Australia and China (e.g., common law vs. civil law).
Evaluate how historical, socio-political, economic, and cultural factors shape different legal systems and substantive norms in Australia and China.
Identify the relative strengths, weaknesses, and functional equivalents of legal concepts across the Australian and Chinese jurisdictions.

Independently locate, interpret, and evaluate primary and secondary legal sources (e.g., constitutions, statutes, and jurisprudence) from Australia and China on fundamental commercial law matters affecting business in Australia and China.
Articulate complex comparative legal analyses and theories to both specialist (legal practitioners) and non-specialist (client) audiences, both orally and in writing.

assessments.task_description:
By the end of the seminar in Week 2, students will form a small group (3-5 students). You will be given opportunities for group formation during the seminars in Weeks 1 and 2, and you may choose your own group members.

 

Your assessment task will be a group presentation.

 

Your group will choose one of the 8 seminar topics addressed during this course. During the group presentation, you will discuss the chosen topic, explain your research and present your ideas as to how the laws and regulations of Australia and China compare in regard to the chosen topic.

 

The assessment is designed to test the following skills and competencies:

 

  1. Legal research — the ability to identify and engage with primary legal sources (legislation, regulations, administrative guidance) in both jurisdictions.
  2. Comparative legal analysis — the ability to identify meaningful similarities and differences between the two legal systems as they apply to the chosen topic.
  3. Issue identification and prioritisation — the ability to identify the most material legal issues from a practical perspective and to exercise sound judgement in prioritising those issues.
  4. Oral presentation — the ability to communicate complex legal matters clearly, concisely, and in an oral presentation format appropriate to the context.
  5. Application — the ability to apply general legal principles to the specific topic within a group context.


Due date

The group presentation will take place in Week 5.

 In class, you will be shown how to book a timeslot for your group presentation.

 

Format

All group members must be present for the presentation. Your group will be presenting to the course coordinator and fellow students.

You may use a single page handout to support your in-person presentation. Powerpoint presentations are not allowed.

 

Group input

All group members must contribute to selecting, developing, researching, and presenting the topic. The oral component of the presentation will be shared among group members, and all group members must be prepared to answer questions during the Q&A component.


Every group member will receive the same mark, unless a case is made otherwise. Two assessors will review the presentation to provide moderation of grades.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description: Within five days of your oral presentation, you will submit an INDIVIDUAL reflections on (a) how the what you have learnt in class will impact how you undertake your career; and (b) your experiences undertaking the group oral presentation (including reflection on your topic choice, your contribution to the work, and the group work process). Further guidance will be provided in class.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Reflections must be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will be given a scenario during the Week 3 seminar. Based on the scenario, students will be required to prepare a legal memorandum of no more than 2,200 words. The memorandum will need to follow a professional legal memorandum format. Students will be required to use clear, numbered or lettered headings and subheadings. The memorandum should be written in a professional legal register. It should be concise, analytical, and practical.

 

The legal memorandum will be due at 2.00pm on Friday of Week 7.

 

Referencing must comply with the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition). All legislative provisions, cases, and secondary sources cited must appear in footnotes only. Font: Times New Roman or Arial, 12-point body text, 10-point footnotes, 1.5 line spacing.

 

This assessment must be the student's own individual work.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignment must be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
